title: RegistryWarning type widened to {kind: string}, breaks EngineWarning discrimination
description: RegistryWarning is declared as `{ kind: string; message: string; ... }` rather than a discriminated union of literal-typed variants (path-malformed, paths-empty, paths-missing, missing-path-json, malformed-path-json, invalid-path-json, missing-phases-json, malformed-phases-json, invalid-phases-json). Because RegistryWarning is a member of the EngineWarning union and accepts any string for `kind`, every EngineWarning effectively reduces to `{ kind: string; message: string }` at the discriminator level — narrowing such as `if (w.kind === 'state-corrupt')` no longer reliably narrows away other variants. The narrower interfaces declared above it (PathMalformedWarning, PathsEmptyWarning, PathsMissingWarning) are dead code: registry.ts pushes plain object literals typed as RegistryWarning rather than those interfaces, and the literal kinds those interfaces use (`path-malformed` etc.) don't actually appear in the runtime wire output (registry emits `malformed-path-json`, `empty-paths-dir`, `no-paths-dir`).
impact: Defeats the stated goal of A20 — a 'unified discriminated union'. Future code that switches on EngineWarning.kind cannot exhaustively check; the consolidator/cycle-4 caller will silently lose cases. The dead PathMalformedWarning/PathsEmptyWarning/PathsMissingWarning interfaces also create reader confusion: they look authoritative but are never instantiated.
recommendation: Replace RegistryWarning with an actual discriminated union of the kinds registry.ts emits (`'no-paths-dir' | 'empty-paths-dir' | 'missing-path-json' | 'malformed-path-json' | 'invalid-path-json' | 'missing-phases-json' | 'malformed-phases-json' | 'invalid-phases-json'`), drop the unused PathMalformedWarning/PathsEmptyWarning/PathsMissingWarning interfaces (or rename the active ones to match the wire kinds), so EngineWarning is a real discriminated union end-to-end.

title: Deploy executor lives in probes/manifest.ts; should be its own deploy.ts module
description: manifest.ts mixes two distinct responsibilities: (a) a passive read-only probe (`probeSandboxManifestReachable`, ~20 LOC) and (b) a non-trivial subprocess executor (`runDeployRemediation`, `runStubDeploy`, `runRealDeploy`, ~140 LOC) that spawns processes, manages timeouts, captures logs, and re-probes. The contract names this as 'the deploy executor lives in this file' but doing so makes manifest.ts the only probe file that owns active state-changing behavior. Every other probe file is a single ~20-50 LOC pure check; manifest.ts is ~185 LOC with two unrelated public surfaces. The file boundary obscures that the deploy executor is callable by the tool handler independent of the probe's pass/fail outcome, and the colocation forces the tool handler (`runPreflightProbe.ts`) to import `runDeployRemediation` from `probes/manifest.js` — leaking the probe abstraction.
impact: Future maintainers reading probes/manifest.ts have to mentally separate 'is this part of the probe or part of the executor?'. Tests that mock the manifest probe accidentally widen their reach to the executor (and vice-versa). The asymmetry — 7 probe files at ~30 LOC and one at ~185 LOC — is a code-smell signal that the boundary is wrong.
recommendation: Extract `runDeployRemediation`, `runStubDeploy`, `runRealDeploy`, `DeployRemediationResult`, and the `DEPLOY_TIMEOUT_MS`/`MAX_RECHECK_ATTEMPTS` constants into `mcp/server/src/probes/deploy.ts` (or `mcp/server/src/deploy.ts` if you accept it isn't a probe). manifest.ts then exposes only the probe and the `getSandboxCwd` helper (or moves that helper to deploy.ts). runPreflightProbe.ts imports `runDeployRemediation` from `deploy.js` instead of `probes/manifest.js`, which reads as: 'probe failed, hand off to the deploy module' — clearer than 'tool handler reaches into the manifest probe's internals'.

title: Module-level setSpawnOverride Map is a test seam in production code
description: preflight.ts owns a module-private `Map<ProbeId, SpawnFn>` populated through the exported `setSpawnOverride` API. The Map is global mutable singleton state in the production module whose only consumer is `scripts/e2e/harness.ts` (a test harness). The synthesis-notes called out competing workers for putting `vi.spyOn` in production code; worker-4's choice avoided that, but replaced it with a different test seam — module-level mutable state with no production caller. The override mechanism also silently no-ops for probes that don't read `opts.spawn` (probes/node, probes/manifest, probes/suiPilot, probes/sandboxRepo, probes/learningOutputStyle): calling `setSpawnOverride('sui-pilot-enabled', fn)` installs an entry that's never read.
impact: (a) Production binary ships with a test-only API exported as part of the public preflight surface. A future consumer of `runProbe` could mistake setSpawnOverride for a real injection point. (b) Silent no-op overrides on the 5 probes that don't accept `spawn` will create maddening 'why is my stub not firing?' debugging sessions. (c) Global mutable Map in an async server is a footgun if/when concurrent tool calls land — one test's override leaks into a parallel call.
recommendation: Push the spawn injection back into `ProbeOptions` and pass it explicitly through `runProbe(probeId, { spawn })`. The harness fixtures can store the SpawnFn in their own closure and pass it via the tool args (extending `runPreflightProbe` to accept a private testing param) or — cleaner — invoke the probe functions directly in tests without going through `runProbe`. If the global Map must stay, add a runtime guard: `setSpawnOverride` throws when called with a probeId whose probe doesn't accept `spawn` (currently node/manifest/suiPilot/sandboxRepo/learningOutputStyle).

title: defaultSpawn duplicated verbatim across docker.ts, pnpm.ts, suiCli.ts
description: An identical 18-line `defaultSpawn` helper (wrapping `spawnSync` with utf8 encoding, default 5s timeout, error rethrow, and a normalized return shape) is copy-pasted into `probes/docker.ts`, `probes/pnpm.ts`, and `probes/suiCli.ts`. The three copies are character-for-character identical aside from import order. Three places to update if the spawn timeout or error-handling policy changes; three places where a bug fix has to land.
impact: Drift risk: a future patch to one copy (e.g. capturing exit signals, raising the timeout, adding env scrubbing) will be forgotten on the other two. Reviewer mental load is also tripled — three near-identical-but-not-confirmed-identical blocks must be diffed.
recommendation: Extract `defaultSpawn` (and the SpawnFn return type that already lives in preflight.ts) into a small `mcp/server/src/probes/spawnUtil.ts`. The three probe files import `defaultSpawn` from there. Probe files shrink to ~20 LOC each, which matches the single-responsibility shape of node.ts/sandboxRepo.ts/etc.

title: ProbeOptions.remediate is part of the probe interface but no probe reads it
description: `ProbeOptions` is declared as `{ spawn?: SpawnFn; remediate?: boolean }` and threaded through `runProbe` and every per-probe function signature. But none of the eight probes consult `opts.remediate` — the remediate decision is taken entirely in `tools/runPreflightProbe.ts` after the probe returns. The `remediate` field is dead concern in the probe-level abstraction. Worse, the manifest probe ignores `remediate` and always attaches the ShellAction on the fail path (correct per A8/A11 — the action is metadata, not an action trigger), which means `remediate` in ProbeOptions is documented to mean nothing at the probe layer.
impact: The ProbeOptions type misleads readers: a future probe author may believe they're supposed to branch on `opts.remediate`, leading to inconsistent behavior across probes. The contract also explicitly says (A11) that `remediate` is the tool-layer's concern, so leaking it into ProbeOptions confuses the layering.
recommendation: Drop `remediate` from `ProbeOptions`. Probes only need spawn injection. The tool handler already owns the remediate gate. If ProbeOptions ends up containing only `spawn?`, consider renaming to `SpawnInjection` or inlining it as a single optional parameter.

title: Redundant fs.fsyncSync after handle.sync() preserved purely to satisfy a stale test spy
description: saveState calls `await handle.sync()` then immediately calls `fs.fsyncSync(handle.fd)`. The inline comment explains: 'Redundant but required for T-061's fs.fsync* spy ordering assertion.' This is a smell in two directions: (a) production code has a no-op syscall whose only justification is a test from cycle 2 still asserting that path, and (b) the comment confirms the cycle-2 fix bundle (C002+C007+C009+C027) was incomplete — the migration to FileHandle.sync() should have updated T-061 to spy on the new path rather than keep the old fs.fsyncSync call alive.
impact: Two real-world consequences: the redundant sync executes a second blocking flush per saveState (negligible perf, but non-zero), and any future maintainer reading state.ts has to grok why a Node API is called after its modern replacement. More importantly, the pattern signals 'tests dictate production shape' which is the inverse of what TDD should produce.
recommendation: Update T-061 in tests/state.test.ts to spy on `handle.sync` (the modern path) rather than `fs.fsyncSync`, then drop the `fs.fsyncSync(handle.fd)` line in production. The unused `import * as fs from 'node:fs';` at the top of state.ts can be removed once that's the only fs.* reference. Carry-forward to cycle 4.

title: RunPreflightProbeResult and DeployRemediationResult duplicate the same response shape
description: Two near-identical result types coexist: `RunPreflightProbeResult { pass; message; action?; logs?; warning? }` in tools/runPreflightProbe.ts and `DeployRemediationResult { pass; message; logs?; warning? }` in probes/manifest.ts. The handler explicitly destructures one to construct the other (`return { pass: deployResult.pass, message: deployResult.message, ...(deployResult.logs ? ... : {}), ...(deployResult.warning ? ... : {}) }`). The shapes diverge only in that RunPreflightProbeResult includes `action?` (always undefined on the deploy path) — so the destructure is a structural identity transform.
impact: Two types that mean the same thing means two places to update when a new field is added (e.g. cycle-4 might add `attempt: number`). The destructure-and-rebuild pattern in the handler also clouds the data flow; the reader has to verify by hand that no field is dropped.
recommendation: Hoist a single `PreflightOutcome` type into `warnings.ts` (or a new `mcp/server/src/preflightTypes.ts`) and have both the tool handler and the deploy executor return it. The handler then becomes a passthrough on the remediate path: `return await runDeployRemediation(...)`.

title: Tool handler reaches into probes/manifest.ts for the deploy executor — leaks probe abstraction
description: runPreflightProbe.ts is the generic per-probe tool handler — it should be probe-agnostic. But it directly imports `runDeployRemediation` from `../probes/manifest.js` and applies it whenever the probe result has an attached ShellAction. The coupling makes the tool handler conceptually aware of one specific probe's secondary behavior. If a future probe (say, a probe #9 that offers `pnpm install`) attaches a different shell action with a different remediation, the tool handler would either grow a per-probe switch or pretend that one executor handles all shell remediations.
impact: The probe-registry abstraction (each probe is a uniform `(opts) => ProbeResult`) leaks at the tool handler. The handler's name and contract say 'run a probe'; in practice it also conditionally runs deploy. Adds a hidden per-probe coupling the registry was supposed to avoid.
recommendation: Push the remediation executor into the probe interface itself: extend `ProbeFn` (or add a sibling `RemediationFn`) so each probe optionally exposes `remediate(action) => Promise<RemediationResult>`. The tool handler becomes: `if (remediate && result.action) return probe.remediate(result.action)`. Probes without a remediation simply don't expose the function. The handler is then truly probe-agnostic. (Smaller alternative: keep the current shape but add a comment in runPreflightProbe.ts explicitly acknowledging that today only manifest has remediation, and that the hard-coded import is deliberate-for-now.)

title: registry.ts and tools/start.ts re-export warning types creating two import paths
description: After unification, `registry.ts` does `import type { RegistryWarning } from './warnings.js'; export type { RegistryWarning };` and `tools/start.ts` does the analogous re-export of `StateWarning`. This means future consumers can import `RegistryWarning` from either `./registry` or `./warnings`, and `StateWarning` from either `./tools/start` or `./warnings`. The contract calls for unification into `warnings.ts`; preserving the legacy import paths via re-export is a migration aid that should be deleted in cycle 4 once references are updated, otherwise the dual-source-of-truth defeats unification's purpose.
impact: Slight; today nothing breaks. Going forward, code review can't easily tell whether `import { RegistryWarning } from '../registry'` reflects an old habit or an intentional choice. The unification benefit (single discoverable home for the union) is partially undone.
recommendation: In cycle 4, drop the `export type { RegistryWarning };` from registry.ts and the analogous line from tools/start.ts; update any remaining importers to point at `warnings.js`. For this cycle, optionally add a `// TODO(cycle-4): drop re-export` comment so the cleanup intent is visible.
evidence:
import type { RegistryWarning } from './warnings.js';

export type { RegistryWarning };

title: Three orphan warning interfaces in warnings.ts encode wire kinds the registry doesn't actually emit
description: warnings.ts declares `PathMalformedWarning` with `kind: 'path-malformed' | 'malformed-path-json'`, `PathsEmptyWarning` with `kind: 'paths-empty' | 'empty-paths-dir'`, and `PathsMissingWarning` with `kind: 'paths-missing' | 'no-paths-dir'`. The `path-malformed`/`paths-empty`/`paths-missing` literals are listed in T-171 as expected wire values, but registry.ts only ever emits `malformed-path-json`, `empty-paths-dir`, and `no-paths-dir` (the second alternative in each pair). The first alternative in each union is dead — and tests/registry fixtures (per T-174) confirm the wire shape is the second alternative. So the union members are oversized: each encodes both a phantom kind and the actual one. Compounded by R2-001 (the wide `RegistryWarning`), these three interfaces are also unreferenced — registry.ts pushes object literals typed by RegistryWarning, not any of these named interfaces.
impact: Reader confusion: the file purports to be the source of truth for warning shapes, but three of its declarations are never instantiated and encode kind values that don't exist in the wire payload. T-171 (which only checks the literal substrings appear in the file) passes regardless, masking the dead code.
recommendation: Either (a) remove `PathMalformedWarning`, `PathsEmptyWarning`, `PathsMissingWarning` and let the discriminated `RegistryWarning` (per R2-001's recommendation) carry only the emitted kinds, or (b) keep them but trim the union literals to the kinds registry.ts actually uses (drop `path-malformed`, `paths-empty`, `paths-missing`) and have registry.ts's warnings array typed as `(PathMalformedWarning | PathsEmptyWarning | PathsMissingWarning | …)[]`.
